article.title: Cegedim Santé Breach Exposes Medical Records of 15 Million French Citizens, Including Sensitive Physician Annotations
article.summary: A cyberattack on French healthcare software provider Cegedim Santé has compromised 15.8 million administrative files, with 165,000 containing sensitive physician annotations revealing patients' HIV status, sexual orientation, and other private details.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A cyberattack against French healthcare software provider Cegedim Santé has exposed the administrative records of approximately 15 million patients across France, in what ranks among the country's largest medical data breaches. The breach, which occurred in late 2025, was publicly disclosed on February 27, 2026, after months of investigation by French authorities.

The incident is particularly alarming because a subset of approximately 165,000 stolen files contained free-text physician annotations with highly sensitive personal information, including patients' HIV status, sexual orientation, religious background, and details of sexual assaults.

## What We Know

Cegedim Santé develops MonLogicielMedical (MLM), a practice-management platform used by approximately 3,800 doctors in France. According to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/03/french_medical_leak), the attackers compromised data from roughly 1,500 of those practices, stealing 15.8 million administrative files in the process.

The bulk of the stolen data consists of standard patient administrative information: names, genders, dates of birth, telephone numbers, postal addresses, and email addresses. However, the breach's severity is compounded by the 165,000 files that contained physicians' clinical notes, as reported by [France 24](https://www.france24.com/en/live-news/20260227-hackers-steal-medical-details-of-15-million-in-france). These free-text annotation fields, intended for doctors' internal use, included details such as whether a patient was HIV-positive, their sexual orientation, cases of infidelity, and records of sexual assaults.

French Health Minister Stéphanie Rist confirmed that structured medical records, including prescriptions and biological examination results, were not compromised, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/03/french_medical_leak). Cegedim Santé detected what it described as "abnormal application request behaviour on user doctor accounts" in late 2025 and filed a criminal complaint in October 2025. The Paris prosecutor's office subsequently launched an investigation into "attacks on an automated data system" in November 2025.

The breach was not made public until [France 24](https://www.france24.com/en/live-news/20260227-hackers-steal-medical-details-of-15-million-in-france) reported it on February 27, 2026, roughly four months after Cegedim first notified CNIL, France's data protection authority. Reports indicate that the leaked data includes records belonging to political figures, including potential candidates for France's 2027 presidential election, senior civil servants, and national security officials.

## What We Don't Know

The identity of the attackers remains undisclosed. The alleged hackers told French broadcaster France 2 that only partial information had been published online, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/03/french_medical_leak), but it is unclear whether the full dataset has been sold or distributed through other channels.

The precise attack vector has not been publicly detailed. It remains unknown whether the breach resulted from a vulnerability in the MLM software itself, compromised credentials, or another method. Cegedim Santé has not disclosed the full technical circumstances of the intrusion.

The four-month gap between the October 2025 criminal complaint and the February 2026 public disclosure raises questions about France's breach notification timeline, though the specific regulatory requirements under GDPR's 72-hour notification rule apply to supervisory authority notification rather than public disclosure.

## Context

The breach arrives at a particularly awkward moment for Cegedim Santé. In September 2024, CNIL fined the company 800,000 euros for illegally processing health data. That enforcement action found that Cegedim Santé had been collecting pseudonymized patient data through its software for commercial research purposes without proper authorization, and had misclassified the data as anonymous when patients could in fact be re-identified through unique identifiers and detailed personal health information.

France has faced a series of significant healthcare data breaches in recent years. In early 2024, breaches at health insurance processors Viamedis and Almerys exposed data belonging to 33 million French citizens. The Cegedim Santé incident, while smaller in raw numbers, is arguably more damaging due to the nature of the exposed physician annotations.

The breach underscores a persistent vulnerability in healthcare IT systems: unstructured free-text fields that clinicians use for internal notes can contain some of the most sensitive information in a patient's record, yet they often fall outside the scope of standard data protection controls designed for structured database fields.
